questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = A] How many cats does it take to coordinate a nap in the sun?

options.A: All of them, napping in parallel.
options.B: Exactly one, dispatching to the others.

questions.explanation:
A: Correct — cats nap in parallel. Parallel execution is the canonical pattern.
B: Cats do not accept dispatch roles.
C: Sequential cats miss the sun window.
D: Lone-napping cats are a minority.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = B] Two dogs are sent to fetch the same ball. What happens?

options.B: Race condition; exactly one wins the ball.

questions.explanation: B: Fetch is a classic race. Only one dog can hold the ball — the canonical race-condition pattern.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = C] A butler agent needs to warm tea, polish silver, and answer the door. What is the best orchestration?

options.A: Polish silver first, it is most important.
options.B: Wait for the kettle before starting anything else.
options.C: Run all three concurrently — they are independent.
options.D: Refuse all three and take a walk.

questions.explanation:
A: Prioritization without dependency is unhelpful.
B: The kettle has nothing to do with the door.
C: Correct — independent tasks should run concurrently.
D: Tempting but not orchestration.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = D] A subordinate pigeon returns with stale breadcrumbs. What should the coordinator do?

options.A: Accept the stale crumbs; pigeons are always right.

questions.explanation: D: Stale results → retry with a fresh worker is the canonical pattern.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = A] A flock of geese independently votes for the pond that already has the most geese. What is the expected outcome?

options.A: They consolidate at one pond via positive feedback (rich-get-richer).
options.B: They split evenly across all three ponds.
options.C: They never decide and fly forever.
options.D: Two ponds tie and the third is ignored.

questions.explanation:
A: Correct — positive-feedback dynamics produce one winner pond quickly.
B: Even split needs coordination that doesn't exist.
C: Feedback loops terminate fast in practice.
D: Ties are unstable under this voting rule.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = B] Minimum cooks to spoil the broth?
questions.explanation: B: Proverbial wisdom — too many cooks spoil the broth. Coordination overhead is the bug.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = C] Three retriever subagents return — two balls, one stick. What should the coordinator do?

options.A: Accept the stick — variety is good.
options.C: Return the majority result: a ball.

questions.explanation: C: Correct — majority vote is the standard consensus pattern.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = D] 4 of 5 subagents succeed; the 5th (summarizer) times out. What is the safest recovery?

options.D: Retry only the summarizer with the existing 4 results.

questions.explanation:
A: Over-escalation — partial state is recoverable.
B: Wasteful re-execution of successful work.
C: Ignoring a failure is unsafe.
D: Correct — the 4 successes are still valid; only the summary needs retry.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = A] Your oven is smoking. What is the first command to run?
questions.explanation: A: Safety first. Turn off the source before anything else.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = B] Which of these is a valid slash command?
questions.explanation: B: Correct — slash commands begin with a slash. A is a shell command, C is prose, D is emoji diplomacy.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = C] Where should a team-shared /deploy-lunch command live?

options.C: In the project's .claude/commands/ folder, committed to git.

questions.explanation:
A: Personal commands aren't shared.
B: Sticky notes don't version-control.
C: Correct — commands committed to the project are available to every teammate.
D: Ovens are output-only.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = D] Which mechanism controls automatic cleanup of stale leftovers?
questions.explanation: D: Correct — hooks are the harness's mechanism for running shell commands on lifecycle events, including cleanup.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = A] Disallow /season-with-salt for apprentices but allow for head chefs. How?

options.A: Permission settings scoped per-role in settings.json.

questions.explanation: A: Correct — scoped permissions are the proper mechanism. Trust and hiding are not access controls.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = B] You type /help. What happens?
questions.explanation: B: /help is a built-in CLI affordance.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = C] Which hook event fires before a tool is invoked?
questions.explanation: C: Correct — PreToolUse is the canonical pre-invocation hook name in Claude Code.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = D] Lead chef wants /simmer team-shared and /taste personal-only. Where do they live?

options.A: Both in the project .claude/commands/, shared via git.
options.B: Both in the chef's personal folder.
options.D: /simmer in .claude/commands/ (committed); /taste in settings.local.json or user-level dir (not committed).

questions.explanation:
A: Committing /taste clutters teammates' setups.
B: Personal placement hides /simmer from the team.
C: Ovens do not host commands.
D: Correct — team vs personal separation.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = A] Which prompt gets your dog to sit?
questions.explanation: A: Short, direct, unambiguous instructions work best — for dogs and for LLMs.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = B] What's wrong with the prompt 'Do the thing we talked about'?

options.B: It's ambiguous — no referent for 'the thing'.

questions.explanation: B: Correct — ambiguous references are a top failure mode. Spell out the task.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = C] Best structure for asking for 3 items, each with name + description?

options.C: JSON schema with items[].name and items[].description.

questions.explanation:
A: Too vague.
B: Riddles aren't schemas.
C: Correct — explicit schema with named fields is the structured-output pattern.
D: Haiku is underspecified.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = D] A prompt gives wildly different answers on repeated runs. Likely fix?
questions.explanation: D: Correct — low temperature makes sampling more deterministic.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = A] Model keeps inventing a 4th category when you want exactly 3. Strongest fix?

options.A: Constrained output schema with an enum of the 3 valid values.
options.D: Ignore the extra category in post-processing.

questions.explanation:
A: Correct — enum-constrained output prevents invalid values at generation. Strongest guarantee.
B: Few-shot helps but doesn't structurally prevent invention.
C: Politeness doesn't constrain output.
D: Fixes symptoms, not cause.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = B] Is 'You are a helpful assistant.' a useful system prompt?

options.B: It's fine but adds little information.

questions.explanation: B: Generic helpful-assistant framing is harmless but shouldn't substitute for a specific, scoped prompt.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = C] Most reliable way to have the model think step-by-step?

options.C: Extended thinking / chain-of-thought with a scratch area.

questions.explanation: C: Correct — giving the model explicit room to reason is the standard CoT pattern.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = D] Summarization works on short docs but not a 200-page PDF. First direction?

options.D: Chunk the document, summarize each, then summarize the summaries (map-reduce).

questions.explanation: D: Correct — map-reduce over chunks is the canonical long-document summarization pattern.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = A] Which tool to drive in a nail?
questions.explanation: A: Tool-to-task fit is the most important MCP design principle.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = B] Designing a hammer tool — what type should `target` be?
questions.explanation: B: Specific typed fields beat generic objects.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = C] Hammer tool returns 'error: nail missing'. What should the caller do?

options.A: Retry immediately in a tight loop.
options.C: Surface the error and skip the hammering step.

questions.explanation:
A: Tight retry is a DoS pattern.
B: Silent failures are the worst kind.
C: Correct — surface the failure, don't hide it.
D: Denial is not a strategy.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = D] `hammer` and `mjolnir` both hit nails (mjolnir also summons thunder). Which should the server expose?

options.B: Only mjolnir — thunder is always helpful.
options.D: Both, with clear descriptions so the agent picks appropriately.

questions.explanation: D: Correct — expose the menu, write clear descriptions, let the agent choose.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = A] 30-second drill tool is saturated by agent calling it in a loop. Best fix?

options.A: Add a batch-drill tool that accepts multiple holes in one call.

questions.explanation:
A: Correct — batched operations reduce per-call overhead.
B: Not an API-design fix.
C: Removes a valid capability.
D: Doesn't address saturation.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = B] Which is the worst tool name?
questions.explanation: B: Correct — `do_stuff` tells the agent nothing about when to call it.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = C] Tool description says 'Use when the user wants a thing.' Problem?

options.C: Too vague for the agent to know when to call it.

questions.explanation: C: Descriptions drive tool selection. Vague descriptions cause wrong-tool calls.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = D] Safest default for a `delete_database` MCP tool?

options.A: Execute immediately — agents know what they're doing.
options.C: Log each call and hope for the best.
options.D: Require a confirm=true parameter AND return a dry-run diff before real deletion.

questions.explanation:
A: Destructive defaults should never be immediate.
B: Misleading naming is a security antipattern.
C: Logging after destruction is too late.
D: Correct — explicit confirm + dry-run is the canonical safety pattern.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = A] You forgot what's on your grocery list. Fastest fix?

options.A: Go home and read it — the list is the source of truth.

questions.explanation: A: Reading the list costs little; buying everything costs a lot.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = B] List says 'eggs, milk, bread'. You bought eggs. What should the tracker remember?
questions.explanation: B: Correct — incremental state updates, minimal relevant context.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = C] Tracker's grocery list grew to 400 items because it never forgets. Problem?

options.C: Context bloat — retaining items that are no longer relevant.
options.D: Nothing; more context is always better.

questions.explanation: C: Correct — indefinite context growth without pruning is a classic failure mode.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = D] Shopping trip is 20 min and the list changes twice. How to keep the agent in sync?

options.A: Restart the agent on every change.
options.D: Pass the updated list as part of every next turn's context.

questions.explanation: D: Correct — fresh state in every turn's context is the canonical way to keep an agent current.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = A] Two agents share a notebook. A buys eggs; B buys eggs 30s later because it hadn't re-read. Bug category?
questions.explanation: A: Correct — classic stale-read race condition. Needs locking, versioning, or fresher reads.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = B] Context window almost full. Drop what first?
questions.explanation: B: Correct — drop the oldest low-relevance content first.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = C] Simplest way to avoid reloading the full cookbook every turn?

options.C: Cache relevant sections and reference them by name.

questions.explanation: C: Correct — selective caching + reference by handle keeps large material outside the hot context.

questions.stem: [DEMO · correct = D] Long-running agent crashes and restarts. What to persist?

options.D: Durable facts (goal, completed steps, known state); drop ephemeral bookkeeping.

questions.explanation:
A: Over-persistence wastes storage and replays irrelevant history.
B: Loses all progress.
C: Insufficient for resume.
D: Correct — durable vs ephemeral is the right axis.
